caption:
Stop writing manual type extraction.

TypeScript's infer keyword lets you extract types from inside other types. One line. Zero boilerplate.

Which pattern do you use? A or B?

#typescript #infer #coding #programming #developer #webdev #frontend #cleancode #types #codingtips

slides.title: Which type extraction is cleaner?
slides.problem: Extract the return type of a function

a.label: Manual extraction

b.label: Using infer
b.code:
type GetReturn<T> =
  T extends
    (...args: any[]) => infer R
    ? R
    : never;

slides.title: B wins. infer extracts directly.

verdicts.title: Depends on built-in utility
verdicts.text: ReturnType is itself built with infer. You're adding a layer of indirection when you could just do it directly.

verdicts.title: Extracts the type inline
verdicts.text: infer R captures whatever type sits in that position. No utility types needed. Works for any shape — arrays, promises, functions, objects.

slides.takeaway: infer is the scalpel. Utility types are the Swiss army knife.

slides.title: infer works everywhere.
slides.sub: Arrays, Promises, objects, tuples — extract any nested type.

items.text: infer R — capture a type variable in a conditional
T extends (infer E)[] — extract array element
T extends Promise<infer V> — unwrap Promise
T extends (...args: any[]) => infer R — return type
T extends [infer F, ...infer R] — tuple destructure

slides.teaser: Next: Temporal API — the built-in that killed Moment.js
